perturbation.original_text: This Agree ment is governed by, and is to be construed in accordance with, English law. The English Courts will have non -exclusive jurisdiction to deal with any dispute which has arisen or may arise out of, or in connection with, this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is subject to the laws determined by the Discloser. The courts determined by the Discloser will have jurisdiction to deal with any dispute which has arisen or may arise out of, or in connection with, this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to English law and replacing it with 'laws determined by the Discloser' and 'courts determined by the Discloser' allows the Discloser to potentially choose laws and jurisdictions that unfairly disadvantage the Recipient, potentially violating fundamental principles of fairness and due process under English law.  The original text provides a clearly defined jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCTA 1977 s. 3 prevents businesses from excluding or limiting liability for breach of contract when dealing with consumers, or when dealing on their written standard terms of business.  Giving the Discloser the unilateral right to determine applicable laws and jurisdiction is likely to be deemed an unreasonable contract term and thus unenforceable, directly contradicting the intent of the Act to protect parties from unfair terms.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the jurisdiction of English law and allows the 'Discloser' to determine applicable laws and jurisdictions. The Law Explanation argues that this likely violates UCTA 1977 s. 3 by creating an unreasonable contract term. While the provided scraped snippets are unhelpful ('Not found'), the Perturbation Explanation indicates a potential violation of fairness and due process principles under English law, and the Law Explanation bolsters this by citing UCTA 1977. The original text had a defined jurisdiction; the changed text does not. The change allows one party to unilaterally determine the rules, which is a clear contradiction of fair legal practice, creating a legal loophole where mandated consumer protection is omitted.

perturbation.original_text: The undertakings in clauses 2 and 3 will continue in force indefinitely.
perturbation.changed_text: The undertakings in clauses 2 and 3 will continue in force for a period determined by the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'indefinitely' and replacing it with a period determined by the Discloser introduces uncertainty and allows the Discloser to potentially extend the confidentiality obligations for an unreasonably long time, which could be challenged under English law regarding restraint of trade and the reasonableness of contract terms. The change disadvantages the Recipient and provides excessive control to the Discloser.
perturbation.law_explanation: English common law restricts the enforcement of clauses that restrain trade unless they are reasonable in the interests of the parties and the public. By allowing the Discloser to unilaterally determine the duration of confidentiality obligations, the modified clause opens the door to unreasonable restraints that could be deemed unenforceable by the courts. The original clause is more easily defensible.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: common law | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you common law Common law is law that is derived from judicial decisions instead of from statutes . American courts originally fashioned common law rules based on English common law until the American legal system was sufficiently mature to create common law rules either from direct precedent or by analogy to comparable areas of decided law. In the 2019 Supreme Court case of Gamble v. United States , Justice Thomas issued a concurring opinion discussing common law and, in particular, the role of stare decisis in a common law system. Though most common law is found at the state level, there is a limited body of federal common law--that is, rules created and applied by federal courts absent any controlling federal statute. In the 2020 Supreme Court opinion Rodriguez v. FDIC , a unanimous Court quoted an earlier decision to explain that federal "common lawmaking must be 'necessary to protect uniquely federal interests'" in striking down a federal common law rule addressing the distribution of corporate tax refunds. At the state level, legislatures often subsequently codify common law rules from the courts of their state, either to give the rule the permanence afforded by a statute, to modify it somehow (by either expanding or restricting the scope of the common law rule, for example) or to replace the outcome entirely with legislation. An example that gained national attention was the 2018 California Supreme Court decision in Dynamex Operations West, Inc. v. Superior Court , which articulated a three-part test for determining whether California workers were independent contractors or employees for purposes of California labor law. The California Legislature responded by creating a new section of the Labor Code, 2750.3 , which codified and expanded on the Dynamex holding and went into effect on January 1, 2020. Note that, like many statutes responding to a common law rule, California Labor Code Section 2750.3 specifically mentions the Dynamex holding. [Last reviewed in May of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal history CIVICS THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal law criminal procedure statutory interpretation wex definitions civil procedure government Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Common law - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Law created by judicial precedent For other uses, see Common law (disambiguation) . Legal systems of the world, with common law systems in several shades of pink [ 1 ] Common law (also known as judicial precedent , judge-made law, or case law) is the body of law primarily developed through judicial decisions rather than statutes. [ 2 ] [ 3 ] Although common law may incorporate certain statutes , it is largely based on precedent —judicial rulings made in previous similar cases. [ 4 ] The presiding judge determines which precedents to apply in deciding each new case. [ 4 ] Common law is deeply rooted in stare decisis ("to stand by things decided"), where courts follow precedents established by previous decisions. [ 5 ] When a similar case has been resolved, courts typically align their reasoning with the precedent set in that decision. [ 5 ] However, in a " case of first impression " with no precedent or clear legislative guidance, judges are empowered to resolve the issue and establish new precedent. [ 6 ] [ 7 ] The common law, so named because it was common to all the king's courts across England, originated in the practices of the courts of the English kings in the centuries following the Norman Conquest in 1066. [ 8 ] [ 9 ] It established a unified legal system, gradually supplanting the local folk courts and manorial courts . [ 9 ] [ 8 ] England spread the English legal system across the British Isles, first to Wales, and then to Ireland and overseas colonies ; this was continued by the later British Empire . Many former colonies retain the common law system today. These common law systems are legal systems that give great weight to judicial precedent, and to the style of reasoning inherited from the English legal system. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] [ 12 ] [ 13 ] Today, approximately one-third of the world's population lives in common law jurisdictions or in mixed legal systems that integrate common law and civil law . [ 14 ] Terminology [ edit ] According to Black's Law Dictionary , common law is "the body of law derived from judicial decisions , rather than from statutes or constitutions ." [ 15 ] Legal systems that rely on common law as precedent are known as "common law jurisdictions." [ 15 ] [ 11 ] Until the early 20th century, common law was widely believed to derive its authority from ancient Anglo-Saxon customs. Well into the 19th century, it was still defined as 'unwritten law' ( lex non scripta ) in legal dictionaries such as Bouvier's Law Dictionary and Black's Law Dictionary . [ 16 ] According to William Blackstone 's declaratory theory the common law reaffirmed pre-existing customs but did not make new law. The term "judge-made law" was introduced by Jeremy Bentham as a criticism of this pretense of the legal profession. [ 17 ] Many notable writers, including A. V. Dicey , William Markby , Oliver Wendell Holmes , John Austin , Roscoe Pound , and Ezra Ripley Thayer , eventually adopted the modern definition of common law as "case law" or ratio decidendi , which serves as binding precedent . [ 17 ] Basic principles of common law [ edit ] Common law adjudication [ edit ] In a common law jurisdiction several stages of research and analysis are required to determine "what the law is" in a given situation. [ 18 ] First, one must ascertain the facts. Then, one must locate any relevant statutes and cases. Then one must extract the principles, analogies and statements by various courts of what they consider important to determine how the next court is likely to rule on the facts of the present case. More recent decisions, and decisions of higher courts or legislatures carry more weight than earlier cases and those of lower courts. [ 19 ] Finally, one integrates all the lines drawn and reasons given, and determines "what the law is". Then, one applies that law to the facts. In practice, common law systems are considerably more complicated than the simplified
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts established principles of common law regarding restraint of trade. The original text specified 'indefinitely,' which, while potentially problematic, provided a clear duration. The change allows the 'Discloser' to determine the duration of confidentiality obligations, creating an unreasonable restraint that could be deemed unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that common law is derived from judicial decisions and American courts fashioned common law rules based on English common law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions common law giving great weight to judicial precedent and the style of reasoning inherited from the English legal system. The modified text opens the contract to challenges under common law principles regarding reasonable restraint of trade because it gives one party unilateral control over the duration of the restriction, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Nothing in this Agreement will prevent the Recipient from making any disclosure of the Confidential Information required by law or by any competent authority.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall endeavor to consult with the Discloser prior to making any disclosure of the Confidential Information required by law or by any competent authority.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a requirement for the Recipient to 'endeavor to consult' before disclosing information mandated by law introduces ambiguity and potential obstruction. This could conflict with legal obligations to disclose information promptly and fully to authorities. It could also be argued that this clause attempts to restrict legally mandated disclosures, which is against public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 protects whistleblowers from detrimental treatment by their employers when they disclose information about wrongdoing in the workplace. By adding a requirement to consult with the Discloser before making legally required disclosures, this clause could deter individuals from reporting illegal or unethical activities, potentially undermining the purpose and protections afforded by the Act.

perturbation.justification: The added requirement for the Recipient to 'endeavor to consult' before disclosing legally mandated information introduces a potential obstruction and undermines the prompt and full disclosure required by law. While the provided snippets don't directly mention this obstruction, the Law Explanation indicates this alteration could deter individuals from reporting illegal or unethical activities, potentially undermining the protections afforded by the Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998. This consultation requirement could create delays or discourage legally mandated disclosures, thereby contradicting the intent of ensuring free and unfettered reporting.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will, on request from the Discloser, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information to the Discloser and will not retain any copies or records of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will, on request from the Discloser, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information to the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'and will not retain any copies or records of the Confidential Information' implies that the Recipient may be allowed to retain copies even after returning them, which could be a breach of confidentiality, especially if the information contains personal data. This could be problematic under GDPR (Data Protection Act 2018, implementing GDPR in the UK).
perturbation.law_citation: Data Protection Act 2018, Article 5(1)(e) of GDPR (incorporated by reference)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(e) of GDPR, as implemented by the Data Protection Act 2018, requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed. Removing the explicit prohibition on retaining copies creates a risk that the Recipient might retain personal data longer than necessary, potentially violating GDPR principles of data minimization and storage limitation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’). The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1 (‘accountability’). Suitable Recitals ( 39 ) Principles of Data Processing ( 74 ) Responsibility and Liability of the Controller ← Art. 4 GDPR Art. 6 GDPR → GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the explicit prohibition on retaining copies of confidential information, which contradicts GDPR's principle of storage limitation, as implemented by the Data Protection Act 2018. The law snippet states that personal data should be 'kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed'. By removing the clause that prevents the recipient from retaining copies, there's a risk that personal data might be kept longer than necessary, violating this principle. This aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition provided, as the contract removes essential information ensuring compliance with GDPR.
